name: Natural Gas / LNG
summary: Day 52 Sunday. RAS LAFFAN PARTIAL-RESTART HORIZON FIRMING — NORTH WITHIN ONE MONTH, SOUTH LATE SUMMER, FULL RECOVERY 3-5 YEARS. QatarEnergy CEO Al-Kaabi (March 20) reiteration holds; The National + OilPrice.com April 9. Cheniere LNG closed Friday $251.07 (-4.4% day, -5.7% month) — likely MISPRICED on short-horizon supply profile; Sunday intel (SNSC institutional ratification + kinetic broadening + MEA summons) argues Friday's -4.4% reverses toward +3-5% Monday. TTF-JKM spread to re-widen; gap-fill thesis validated. European TTF ~€41.25/MWh Friday close (-1.96% day, -26% month from €56 April 13 peak). HH $2.67/MMBtu (+1.1% day) — US domestic unaffected by Gulf crisis, decoupling intact. Asian LNG imports still at 6-year low reducing European competition for cargoes — supporting TTF decline. Structural vs tactical: Cheniere's Friday crash co-moved with Araghchi deal-optimism = market-pricing error on a structural gap-fill trade driven by physical Qatar timelines. Monday reversibility HIGH on Iranian reciprocal measures or SNSC enforcement. (yfinance: LNG $251.07, -4.4% 1D, -5.7% 1M, +24.0% 3M)

price_snapshots.delta_context: European gas was at moderate levels
Surged 45% in single day on Ras Laffan shutdown
Further spike after Iranian missiles caused 5-year damage
Pulled back on peace signal but still +85% from pre-crisis. Goldman forecast EUR 55 for April. EU storage at 30% (five-year seasonal low).

signals.content: QatarEnergy halted LNG production at Ras Laffan (world's largest LNG facility, ~20% of global supply) after attacks. European TTF surged 45%.
Iranian missiles damaged LNG Trains 4 & 6 at Ras Laffan. 17% of Qatar LNG capacity destroyed. Estimated $20B annual revenue loss. 5-year repair timeline. This is STRUCTURAL, not temporary.

European TTF surged 70% in March to EUR54.52/MWh -- worst monthly gain since Sep 2021. Driven by Qatar Ras Laffan destruction (17% capacity, 5-year repair) + Hormuz blocking LNG tankers. European storage at 30% (historically low after harsh winter). Emergency measures in 4 European countries: Spain cut VAT from 21% to 10% on energy, Italy allocated EUR100M support, Slovakia and Slovenia introduced pump rationing. ECB froze rate cuts March 19 and raised inflation forecast. Atlantic Council: crisis 'could trigger European energy crisis' at 2022 levels. If blockade persists through summer refill season (May-Oct), EUR80-100/MWh realistic -- industrial shutdowns begin at that level.

Henry Hub nat gas -4.7% to $2.95 on Monday. US production strong, partially offsetting Gulf disruption. TTF pulled back slightly to EUR54.18 (-1.9%). European storage still critical at 30%. Goldman base case TTF EUR72/MWh in Q2. (Sources: yfinance, OilPriceAPI)

Eurozone inflation surged to 2.5% (Eurostat flash estimate), energy component exploded to +4.9% (from -3.1%), an 8.0 percentage point swing in one month. Most dramatic energy inflation print since 2022 Russia-Ukraine shock. Shell warned 'Europe could face shortage of fuel as early as April.' EU gas storage at ~30% -- lowest seasonal level since before 2022. Some analysts now predict ECB rate HIKES at April and June meetings. Chemical and steel manufacturers imposing surcharges up to 30% with 'permanent deindustrialization' language appearing. After IEA SPR release, only Japan (124 days) meets 90-day reserve requirement -- Germany 76 days, France 70, UK 39, Australia 27. TTF pulled back to EUR53.73 on Pezeshkian peace signal but structural supply loss (Qatar 17% capacity destroyed, 5-year repair) persists. CF Industries -5.6%, Cheniere -3.4% on de-escalation trade. (Sources: Eurostat, Shell, CNBC, Euronews)

CONFIRMED: European TTF gas at EUR 47.51/MWh -- down 6.4% daily on de-escalation hopes, but still elevated at +85% from pre-crisis levels at peak. Goldman Sachs forecast EUR 55/MWh for April. EU gas storage at 30%, the five-year seasonal low. To meet winter storage targets, EU needs to inject 60 bcm by December -- structurally impossible without Hormuz LNG supplies resuming. Shell's April fuel shortage warning is now playing out -- 'we are IN the window Shell warned about.' Slovenia has begun rationing. Spain spending EUR 5B on emergency energy measures. (Sources: TradingEconomics, ICE Endex, Shell)

CONFIRMED: Heating oil +3.7% vs gasoline -7.3% -- SPLIT signal revealing structural distillate crisis. Approximately 10% of global gasoil trade (750K bpd) and ~20% of jet fuel trade (400K bpd) transits Hormuz directly (Kpler data). 1.2M bpd of NW European refining capacity offline for scheduled spring maintenance. EIA showed 5.6M barrel draw in distillate stocks. Distillate crisis is structural even if crude prices normalize -- the refining capacity and product flow disruption persists independently. (Sources: Kpler, EIA)

TTF eased to EUR49.95/MWh (from EUR55.80 peak) -- still +60% from pre-crisis. Structural deficit unchanged: Qatar LNG offline, Ras Laffan 5-year repair. EU storage ~30%. ECB projects 3.1% Q2 inflation driven by energy. The easing is driven by de-escalation hopes (Pezeshkian April 1), not supply improvement -- if April 6 deadline expires without extension, TTF rebounds. Natural gas-to-fertilizer cascade: QAFCO offline -> urea at $750/mt -> India Kharif at risk. (Sources: OilPriceAPI, CNBC, ECB projections)

European TTF gas at EUR 49.98/MWh (-0.6% 1D but +50% WoW). Qatar LNG still offline -- Ras Laffan 5-year repair timeline unchanged. US LNG filling the gap: Cheniere Energy +12.9% monthly, +41.9% 3M ($281.16). Henry Hub at $2.81/MMBtu (flat) -- US domestic market completely decoupled from international prices due to abundant domestic supply and LNG export terminal capacity constraints. ECB raised 2026 inflation forecast to 2.6% driven by energy -- TTF is the transmission mechanism for ECB policy decisions. EU storage still at ~30% (five-year seasonal low). China banned urea exports until August, tightening the natural gas-to-fertilizer cascade further. OPEC+ 206K bpd increase is symbolic -- cannot meaningfully offset LNG supply loss from Qatar. The structural deficit is unchanged: even if ceasefire arrives, Qatar's 17% capacity is destroyed for 5 years.

Henry Hub $2.79/MMBtu (-0.2%, -7.0% 1M, -16.6% 3M) -- flat/falling despite global energy disruption because US natural gas is a DOMESTIC market with limited export infrastructure. LNG export terminals at capacity -- US gas can't reach international market fast enough. European TTF at EUR49.95 reflects the crisis, Henry Hub does not. Henry Hub/TTF divergence widening -- US gas producers benefit from LNG export premium (Cheniere +45.3% 3M, +13.8% 1M) but domestic spot doesn't move. Israel's South Pars strike is significant -- Iran's largest petrochemical plant linked to shared South Pars/North Dome gas field. Qatar's North Dome operations unaffected but precedent of striking shared resource infrastructure escalates risk. (Sources: yfinance, Fortune, FinancialContent)

NATURAL GAS DECOUPLING CONFIRMED -- Henry Hub $2.80/MMBtu, -12.2% 1M while TTF holds EUR 50/MWh and Cheniere LNG +13.8% 1M. US export capacity maxed (Cheniere full); domestic supply intact. Crisis is GLOBAL gas, not US gas. Cheniere captures the LNG export premium; HH does not. Insulation is structural. SOUTH PARS #2 STRIKE + QATAR EXPOSURE: Israel struck the Iranian petrochemical plant inside the world's largest gas field (shared with Qatar). While the gas reservoir was not hit, the precedent is alarming for Qatar's $200B+ LNG expansion. Two Qatar LNG tankers were quietly allowed through Hormuz under a Pakistan-brokered secret deal -- confirming Qatar's acute sensitivity to maintaining LNG flows. Structural risk markets are NOT pricing.

EVENING UPDATE: Henry Hub $2.84/MMBtu (+1.0%), TTF EUR 52.21/MWh (+4.2% 1D, +47% YoY). TTF-HH divergence widening further -- domestic US disconnected from global crisis. Qatar LNG disruption + deadline anxiety driving TTF. EU storage at 28%. BASF +2.7% on 30% price hikes from war-driven gas/feedstock cost pass-through -- proxy for European industrial pricing power. Cheniere +1.0% 1D, +45.3% 3M. South Pars strike on shared field raises Qatar precedent risk. (Sources: ICE Endex, TradingEconomics, CNBC)

NATURAL GAS HENRY HUB -4.3% TO $2.75 -- MUTED REACTION VS BRENT [CONFIRMED]. US natural gas barely moved compared to Brent's -13.5% crash. Mechanism: US natgas is a domestic price; Hormuz reopening doesn't release Henry Hub supply directly. US LNG export economics actually IMPROVE if European TTF stays elevated -- Cheniere structural beneficiary thesis intact. The 4.3% move is mostly demand-recovery anticipation. Cheniere likely down on Wed open but for SECTOR rotation reasons, not commodity-price reasons. European TTF gas remains around EUR49.95-50.40/MWh. If ceasefire holds and Qatar Ras Laffan restart begins (weeks), TTF could fall toward EUR40. Crisis-linked % of natgas move ~30%.

GAS COMPLEX SELL-OFF DEEPENED ON DAY 2 [CONFIRMED]. Henry Hub $2.73 (-4.8% Day 2 vs -4.3% Day 1). European TTF round-trip: crashed -20% to EUR42.50/MWh on initial ceasefire reaction (Qatar Ras Laffan restart hopes), then REBOUNDED back near EUR49.95/MWh as Iran re-closed Hormuz citing Israeli Lebanon strikes. The TTF round-trip is the cleanest physical-market signal that European gas traders did NOT believe the ceasefire would deliver Qatar LNG flows. Cheniere LNG -3.2% on weakened US export economics. Crisis-linked % ~75%. Qatar Ras Laffan still offline since Feb 28. Friday Islamabad outcome will determine whether the round-trip becomes a sustained breakdown (success) or fully retraces (failure).

European TTF gas ~flat at ~EUR44.46/MWh -- down from crisis peaks (~EUR70), still +42% from pre-war ~EUR31. Henry Hub $2.65 (-0.6%). The decoupling persists: US domestic gas disconnected from global crisis. Ceasefire eased LNG supply fears but physical Ras Laffan restart delayed to August (The National, April 9). EU gas storage at 30% (five-year seasonal low) creates summer refill vulnerability -- structurally impossible to meet winter targets without Hormuz LNG resuming. Cheniere -0.1% -- LNG export premium narrowing on TTF decline. Heating oil -5.0% includes seasonal + nat gas substitution + crude pullback. BASF +2.1% (+19.3% 1M) on restructuring -- European chemical sector decoupling from TTF as prices normalize.

Henry Hub at $2.65/MMBtu (-17.5% monthly) -- US domestic story: seasonal demand collapse + record US production + storage surplus + ceasefire LNG relief. TTF at EUR44.46/MWh (down from EUR55 peak but +42% from pre-war EUR31). EU storage at 5-year low ~30% -- summer refill vulnerability. The ceasefire-driven moderation in TTF is at risk if talks failure leads to ceasefire collapse (TTF snaps to EUR55-60). CRITICAL NUANCE: Henry Hub != TTF != JKM. US gas is domestic; war primarily impacts TTF/JKM through LNG supply risks. Henry Hub fell because ceasefire reduced scenario where massive LNG exports would tighten domestic supply. This is the ONE crisis-linked asset moving FAVORABLY for the investor -- cheap US gas feeds through to lower industrial costs. If ceasefire collapses, HH rebounds modestly (+$0.30-0.50) but structural oversupply caps upside. (Sources: EIA, ICE Endex via TradingEconomics, IndexBox)

Henry Hub at $2.61/MMBtu (-0.6% 1D, -16.6% 1M, -16.3% 3M) -- continued decline reflecting US domestic abundance unaffected by Gulf crisis. TTF spiked +7.1% on April 13 blockade day to ~EUR47.14/MWh but decoupling from HH persists. Cheniere $261.60 (-1.5% 1D) but +31.3% 3M -- LNG export beneficiary thesis intact; daily weakness is sector rotation not commodity fundamentals. The HH-TTF spread remains the structural arbitrage opportunity: US gas cheap ($2.61), European gas elevated (EUR47+), Cheniere captures the difference. Container freight (FBX Asia-USWC) at ~$2,400/FEU (+40% since pre-war) adds to LNG shipping costs. EU storage at 30% heading into summer refill (May 1) -- the structural vulnerability that keeps TTF elevated regardless of daily deal headlines. (Sources: markets.md, yfinance)

Henry Hub $2.60 (-0.2% 1D, -14.2% 1M). US domestic supply completely unaffected by Gulf crisis. HH-TTF decoupling persists and widening: HH at $2.60 while TTF crashed -7.5% to €42.95 (largest single-day decline of crisis). Asian LNG imports at 6-year low reducing global competition for supply. TTF decline reflects deal hopes removing LNG supply risk premium. Maersk structural return to Red Sea via MECL service. Natural gas is NOT a crisis-linked trade for the US — the European gas story is TTF-specific and covered under european-ttf-gas node. (Sources: yfinance, ICE Endex)

Day 50. CHENIERE +2.3% COUNTER-DIRECTIONAL WHILE OIL CRASHED -6.5% — US LNG STRUCTURAL BENEFICIARY [CONFIRMED price action]. Cheniere Energy rose 2.3% to $262.74 on the same day Brent crashed -6.5% — a counter-directional move that mirrors CF Industries (+3.2%) in the fertilizer space. The mechanism: even if the Iran war ends and the ceasefire extends, Qatar's Ras Laffan LNG complex is 3-5 years from full repair (The National April 9: North site within a month, South by late summer, but full recovery requires gas turbine procurement with multi-year lead times and $20B annual revenue loss during repairs). US LNG facilities are the structural replacement. HH gas at $2.67 (+1.0%), TTF fell further to EUR 41.62 (-1.9% 1D, -24% 1M) — the TTF decline is driven by Asian LNG imports at 6-year low (reducing European competition) + mild weather + deal hopes, not by Qatar supply recovery. ECB leaning hold today partly because TTF decline gives Lagarde cover. For the investor: Cheniere counter-directional on an oil crash day = the LNG supply chain restructuring is priced as permanent, not crisis-contingent. (Sources: yfinance, The National, TradingEconomics, CNBC)

Day 52. Ras Laffan partial-restart timeline firming: North within one month, South late summer, full recovery 3-5 years per QatarEnergy CEO Al-Kaabi (The National UAE April 9, OilPrice.com April 9, NaturalGasIntel). Cheniere's Friday -4.4% to $251.07 was likely MISPRICED on short-horizon supply profile. Mechanism: Cheniere's structural US LNG gap-fill thesis is driven by Ras Laffan's multi-year repair horizon (Trains 4 & 6 = 12.8 Mtpa = 17% Qatar LNG exports offline, replacement gas turbines face 2-4yr lead times), which is mechanical not political. Araghchi's 'Hormuz completely open' catalyst that crashed Cheniere Friday does NOT address Qatar's physical repair timeline. Sunday SNSC institutional ratification + kinetic broadening + MEA summons = Monday reversal toward +3-5% Cheniere snap-back to ~$260 as Qatar timeline reasserts. TTF-JKM spread to re-widen; gap-fill thesis validated. European TTF €41.25/MWh Friday (-1.96% 1D, -26% 1M from €56 April 13 peak) — deal-optimism deflation; Monday snap-back to €45-50 likely on SNSC re-pricing. HH decoupling intact. (Sources: The National April 9, OilPrice.com April 9, NaturalGasIntel, markets.md Section B, intel.md B2)

edges.context: Day 52. Ras Laffan partial-restart timeline firming: North within one month, South late summer, full recovery 3-5 years per QatarEnergy CEO Al-Kaabi. 17% capacity (Trains 4 & 6 = 12.8 Mtpa) offline; gas turbines face 2-4yr lead times. Cheniere Friday -4.4% likely mispriced on short-horizon supply profile.

LNG shipments from Qatar affected by blockade + Hormuz closure. Even if production resumes, exports blocked by insurance costs + US naval blockade. War risk insurance at prohibitive prices (2.5-5% hull, not suspended per Lloyd's List).
India imports ~60% of LPG. LPG prices hiked. LNG supply disrupted by both Hormuz closure and Ras Laffan damage.

Natural gas is primary feedstock for nitrogen-based fertilizers. HH -16.6% 1M = US domestic fertilizer input costs falling. But global LNG (TTF) still elevated, keeping international fertilizer production costs high.
Oil and gas prices linked. LNG contracts often oil-indexed. Dual energy shock.

trigger_points.mechanism: Would trigger European industrial demand destruction and global fertilizer crisis. India heavily exposed through fertilizer and LPG imports. Currently at EUR47.14 -- needs to more than double. No European energy emergency measures yet (absence signal E5 from intel.md).
